pattern: Feasibility depends only on the counts of zeros and ones, and the cost equals the number of misplaced ones against the chosen alternating pattern, since a single swap fixes two positions.
hints: Only two target patterns exist: one starting with zero and one starting with one. Check which are even possible from the counts of each character.
A swap exchanges any two characters and can fix two wrong positions at once, so the number of swaps for a pattern is the count of positions holding the wrong character at, say, even indices.
If counts allow a pattern, its cost is the number of ones sitting where the pattern wants a zero; take the minimum over the feasible patterns, or return -1 when neither pattern is possible.

edge_inputs.description: all same characters impossible when length above one
odd length forces the majority-start pattern

stress.description: random binary strings, many infeasible, varied length

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def minSwaps(self, s):
        ones = s.count('1')
        zeros = len(s) - ones
        n = len(s)
        def cost(start):
            # pattern[i] = start ^ (i & 1)
            need1 = sum(1 for i in range(n) if (start ^ (i & 1)) == 1)
            if need1 != ones:
                return None
            mis = sum(1 for i in range(n) if int(s[i]) != (start ^ (i & 1)))
            return mis // 2
        candidates = [c for c in (cost(0), cost(1)) if c is not None]
        return min(candidates) if candidates else -1
